And that's it. So that's our best selling thing. It's a piece of paper and a CD. Okay. And it's just simple. So what is the bait that your people would want? One gets them excited. Okay. That's the question you got to start asking yourself. Like what would you be where you're scrolling through Facebook, you're sitting here at night in bed, bored out of your mind, you're scrolling through. What would make you stop and be like, whoa. Okay. Hold on. I got to get to my desktop. Let me click on the link and email it to me so I can go buy that thing right now because I don't want to disappear on my news feed. Okay. It's probably happened to most of you guys at least once. That's what you got to be thinking. Like what is the thing that I'm flipping through Facebook is going to make me stop, get out of my bed, turn my computer on so I can buy this thing. Now some of you guys probably buy on the phone. I don't know how to do that. But I would just go and get the desktop on. Okay. So there you go. All right. So that's funnel number one. Okay. So the second funnel in my sequence, which is my webinar funnel. Okay. So we're moving from tripwire and we're sending people up now to the webinar funnel. So the way it works for me is after somebody's gotten my book, we have about a two week period of time where we're trying to, excuse me, train, educate and just give cool stuff related to the book because I'm waiting for the book to be shipped to somebody. One thing we found back in the day when we had a, we had a big, huge call center is someone ordered a free CD and then we called them, sold them coaching. Before that free CD showed up, it was horrible. Nobody would buy. Why? They got their itched scratched. I spot this thing. Like it's, I already got it. Like I should be fine. Like why are you trying to sell me something new? Like I, that problem has been solved. The CD is in the mail. It's going to be here any day now. Right? But if we waited until the CD got to him and it sat on the desk for a week, week and a half, two weeks and then we call them, guess we could say, have you watched the CD yet? Now it's been sitting on my shelf for like two or three weeks. Like, okay, you really need help. And then we can get him coaching. Right? It's all about kind of timing here. So what we do is we typically, after we go to the tripwire funnel, waiting to get that thing shipped to them so they have access to it so they can get it, they can flip through, they can get excited by it. And then we offer them the next thing. This is where we start the ascension process. Okay? It's about two, two and a half, two to two and a half weeks later. We move in action next from, from action sequence number one, they get dumped now to action sequence number two, which now puts them into our webinar funnel. Okay? Webinar funnel looks kind of like this. And we have a couple of versions. We do these live, like I said, usually once a week. And so this kind of sequence here, we also have an automated one that happens more in the funnels because it's harder to make sure my sequence is right when I'm dropping people all the time. So anyway, you can do automated regular, but this is kind of the overall sequence of the perfect webinar. I'm going to break down each of these pages. Okay? So number one is the registration page. So here's my registration page. How many of you guys saw this page at least once? I mean, you guys looked at that picture. You're like, what is Russell doing? It's so weird. Like he's got his fingers. It's this weird color. And it's just like, he's holding a website, I think. What is that? Is that weird? Okay. And then you have this headline, like my weird niche funnel is currently making me $17,947 per day. Like what is he talking about? Like this is just weird, right? You know, these are the big secrets I found about registration pages. First off, the only thing that matters is this, curiosity. Okay? If people are curious and they cannot figure out what in the world is happening, two things will happen. They will register. And second off, and more important, they will actually show up. Okay? If I'm like, hey, free webinar about how to build a cool sales funnel, they're gonna be like, I think I know what that is. They're just gonna show some things and show me how to build a funnel. If they can figure it out by reading in their head, they may register, but they're definitely not gonna show up. So if you have like, I got a 3% rate at show up rate, it's because they're not curious enough. You read this, you're like, what in the world? A weird niche funnel. Like what niche is it? Like why do you say it's weird? Like what's he selling? Is it 70 grand a day? Like how is that even possible? And I can ethically knock it off. Like how do you ethically knock something off in 10 minutes? I have no idea what he's talking about. The thing in his hand is weird. He's doing this weird like hang loose sign. I gotta figure out like what this is, right? Curiosity is the key, you guys. It's the true on squeeze pages or in registration pages. Like the more curious they are, the more likely they are to come in. Okay? So curiosity is key. Second off, don't do video on your webinar registration page. I've yet to see any cast that I've ever run with video actually won. I've tried hard. I think I'm pretty good at videos too. But I think videos, you give away too much. People sit there thinking like you want some to grab them and just be pure raw curiosity. That's it. So I'm gonna do it again. All capital letters. Curiosity is the secret, you guys. Get curiosity. You do the webinar pitch on your webinar day. A big key is you got to create a really good offer. I'm not going to go to that today, but you create a really good offer and then you do the pitch. So here's some numbers. So let's see. Oh yeah, cash. Remember I put it here. So on a webinar, everyone was asking, what should I be closing out? Like what's a good closing rate, a bad closing rate? So the way that I judge closing rate, right? So some budget-related webinar, some people show up. I go through the perfect webinar. When I transition from the content to the pitch, I'm about to start the stack. I always stop and I look at the number. I look at how many people are currently on right now. And I write that number on a piece of paper. And that's when I'm basing my closing stats on. How many people are on the webinar when I transition to the close? Now the way we look at it is if you were at about a 5% close rate, so if you've got 100 people who are live when you transition to close and you get 5% close rate, that's a good webinar. You can make good money with that. It's something you can run profitably and you'll probably have a pretty good business out of there. If you get to 10% close rate, so you get 100 people, you're going to sell 10 people, that's a million dollar your webinar at least. If you get to 15%, you're looking at a $10 million webinar or more. Those are the stats numbers that I've looked at over the last few years doing this. So that's a metric to look at. You do a webinar, look at your percentage, I'm only at 5%. I need to get it to 8%, to get it to 10%. And from 10 I get to 12% and go up from there. So after the webinar happens, then we need to come back and focus on urgency and scarcity. The big two keys we talked about yesterday a lot. These are the emails to go out, talking about urgency and scarcity. You guys have many handouts, you can see them over there. I will push them into a replay page. This is what my replay page looks like here. In the replay they're able to comment and make questions and things like that. After they've watched the replay, then the next set of emails we're focusing on other things. We had someone transcribe our entire webinar and kind of add the slides back in and we give them the cliff notes of the webinar. So now they can read it. Some people will never watch the webinar, but tons of people will print out and actually we have people literally will print out 100 page transcripts of the webinar and they will read the entire thing. Guess what somebody does after they read 100 page transcripts of your webinar? They give you money. If they're going to sit there and read 100 page transcripts and people do all day long, it's one of the biggest things I learned from Jay Boyer at last year's event was this concept. So we copied it, we modeled it and it's been one of the biggest things to increase conversion rate on the post webinar sequences. We push it into pages where they have the replay plus a link to download the cliff notes. Cliff notes are just PDF that look kind of like that. And then push urgency and scarcity. So as we get closer to the ending date, so for us, usually it's Sunday at midnight we close out sales. So Sunday there's going to be three emails coming out in the morning, day and night basically saying today's the last day, you got 12 hours left, you got three hours left and that last three hour left webinar email is where you make most of the sales. It's crazy. This is a webinar we did last week. Notice the webinar day we hit, I think we had $130,000 on the webinar day and then the day when the cart closed we had $117,000. So if you structured that sequencing right, that's what happened. You kill on the webinar and then the last day you'll make just as much as you do the first day and then you make a bunch of money as well. And this is where the process I started doing is I started trying to build connection with people so that when someone from my team does talk to them on the phone, there's a relationship there. Okay? Like you just just hang up really quick because no one's going to buy at that point. Okay? So this video right here is the connection so they get on like, man, like I trust Russell. That's the question that I want you to be thinking of as you listen to this next individual's story. So ladies and gentlemen, if you would please welcome to the stage Mr. Jacob Hiller. I was thinking about trying to close the room on my vertical jump product, but probably not a good audience. Then I watched the hardcore closer and started hearing the voice. I don't know, maybe I'll try it. They told me to cut it short, but I'm thinking about a close at the end of this, how to jump higher. So my story is not about creating and executing this master plan because unfortunately I didn't really have one when I started off. And knowing what I know now and what actually everybody here knows, I would have done things totally different, completely different. But I just want to share with you how it happened for me and I'm certainly not complaining about how things went down for me and just kind of what I did, how it worked and basically you can have a peek in how somebody stumbled through this. So I've never really strayed from the topic of jumping. People ask me, how did you do your keyword research on jumping? What a cool niche. Well, I never really thought about it. I've always been obsessed with jumping. My parents would tell you I wanted the moon boots and these different jumping things. Ever since I was like eight years old, I've just always loved jumping. I know it seems weird, but I just always loved it. So in 2007, I ended up somehow writing an e-book about how to increase your vertical leap. When I tell people about that, they're like, you mean like jumping higher success in life? I actually mean jumping higher physically. So that's what this is all about. So we've been homeless for eight years now. We started our business while we're on the road and we've lived in about 55 now, about 60 countries. And we run our business fully remotely. And what we do is we carry two bags. One is hers and one is mine. And I have one Xbox, one skateboard, and one blender, of course, Magic Bullet Blender. My son is three. So what I'm kind of asking, before I started my business, I was in the network marketing industry just a little bit. And I was there long enough for somebody to set me down and say, what do you want out of your life? Like, OK, you've got money. What would you do? I would do ABC. I'd buy a car. I'd buy this. Do I want the coolest car? What is it that I want? And that industry made me think about what do I want out of my lifestyle? What am I really trying to achieve? And so I would say for the people in this room who are starting their business or in the business, what is your motivation? What if money were no object? What do you want to do with your life? And find your motivation or find your voice. Like Garrett said, I really like that message of finding and hearing your voice. And what is your breaking point? Maybe you don't think you have a breaking point. Maybe you know what your breaking point is. But think about what that breaking point would be. What if you go months without making a dime and spending money and putting in effort? Is that your breaking point? A year. What is your breaking point? Or do you not have a breaking point? And then get and stay committed to what you're doing. And I believe that this business is so amazing because ultimately it allows us to create whatever lifestyle we want. So it's about finding, you know, know what works and plan to work, work the plan, and then know what doesn't work, which unfortunately a lot of it was for me was finding out what didn't work. And it's pretty, it's nice to finally know that doesn't work, so don't do those things. So I think in the beginning, my greatest strength, the best thing I did, and my brother will tell you I do this very, very well, is I work well with ignorance. I don't know something and I just do it anyways. I don't know, maybe I think I know it or I just move forward despite that. So it seems like the more you fail, the luckier you get. You fail enough, something's going to be lucky. A lot of people, sometimes I just feel like I got lucky. And then I realize I did fail a lot and then eventually, maybe I did just get lucky. I don't know. So the best thing I ever did in my business for sure without a doubt was I took an awkward, very awkward and imperfect step forward. I had people make it fun of me, people in the offices where I worked, looked at my website and they were like, laughed. And later on they're like, I cannot believe that worked. And they're just, you know, so funny. So these are some of my first, awkward first steps. I lived in this dorm room and one day I just was like, I don't know. I really like vertical jump training. I wasn't thinking about a business. I wasn't thinking about making this big or anything. I just said, I'm going to turn on and talk about vertical jump training because I'm fired up about vertical jump training right now. So I propped up the mattress behind my chair and had a green sheet that I just washed and I tried to make a green screen with a stadium behind it. And I put that up thinking it might be convincing. But I was, oh, you can see, I'm literally opaque. You can see through my forehead. Anyway, these silly, that was a big laugh back there. It's not that bad. It's not that bad. Really this channel grew. My videos were terrible. My stage presence on a camera was nervous, extremely white, pale, and just talking about vertical jump training. But that, those steps, people started talking on YouTube. They started saying, we want more. They said, do you have something we can buy? And I'm like, no, no, I don't. Do you have a website? No, no, I don't. Do you have a host? What is that? All these things, I had no idea. There wasn't click funnels. Sometimes I wonder what if I would have had all these tools back then. I really didn't know what I was getting into. I had no website, I had no product, I had no marketing research, I had no plan, and I just had a passion about jumping. So I started sharing my interest and reacting to the audiences and started saying, I need to get a website. And I made the worst website ever. I wish I had a picture of it. It was so bad. But I tried to do it myself, like text file, like coding, like looking up what's HTML, whatever, it was really bad. But that was the start. And I started giving away my product, giving away this PDF that I had wrote. I was scared to charge for it. I was scared to charge for my product. And when I finally did start charging people, I charged them 15 bucks. And then I charged them 30. And then I charged them 67. So when I started my business about, I would say three to six months in, I was making a McDonald's wage. I was making enough that I could probably live on it meagerly if I wanted. But I just went ahead and kept my other job too and just kept working it at the same time. And that's about the same time we went to Germany. Went to Germany because my wife found a job. Things started getting interesting. My wife said, this is actually working. Like can I play too? So we started doing it together. And then went to see a friend in Israel. And from that, we just started working our business and we never stopped traveling. And it's been about eight years. So. Thank you. So the first years of business, you know, it's about content creation. I'm going to put my glasses on because I can't see the screen very well. It was about content creation. I was just doing everything on my own. I was definitely, definitely the bottleneck of my business. But I was still doing it. I was, we weren't living in houses or apartments. We were living in hostels. I mean, literally we walked in. It was a bed and a place to put the suitcases and we were living out of these places. So this is before, and we were trying to use walkie talkies. We didn't even have cell phones. It was really funny. So I was making my content. I was doing all my customer support. I was doing guest interviews. And I'm going to show you exactly some of the things that worked and maybe some of the things that didn't just so you can see what was working for me. And at this point, I kind of like, I was getting that before I was making money and it was exciting. And I was like, wow, this might be something I can do. What is this? And you know, I know what it's like to start a business and not see it moving forward and just wonder, like, is this going to work? Are these efforts in my waste of my time? Should I leave this and try to the next thing? So it's fun to see that energy and that anxiety and that passion and sense of urgency and desperation to some of the people who come here who are just starting their business. And I can't wait to see those people next year and hear their story, how it's going down for them. So here's what I call my hillbilly content marketing. This is just how I did it. There's a lot of better ideas in this room that I've already heard. So this is just what ended up happening for me. So making all this content, I was making like a video a day. Spending time, make a video a day, trying to write an article a day, just doing it all by hand, doing it all on my own. So the great thing about content marketing is it's free. It gets great conversion, potentially lots of sales and great positioning and posturing. The bad thing about it is it can be super time consuming and you're not guaranteed results. So here's my advice and what I ended up doing. End one to three hours per week and here's how I did it. On my YouTube, I get all these questions or I get all these email questions. So I put all of these questions on a big list of Excel spreadsheet or I had a folder on my inbox. I just dragged every single question that I had. So I never made a piece of content unless it addressed a specific individual. So I never thought, oh, this is a good idea. I didn't talk about it. I always addressed a specific individual and I find that that reciprocation seemed to build a sense of community. I answered somebody's question, five other people had it. So you get your list, you answer a question and then you answer another. So what I would do is I would turn on my video and I would say, Joe Blow has a question and I would answer the question and then I'd clap and then I'd say, you know, Susie has a question, answer the question and then I'd clap and I'd do about 10, 20 of those. And in that way, you know, if you look in the video, there's a big spike where you clap. I would just cut them up and then I'd put the intro and outro on and I'd have 20 videos in about an hour. And then I would just schedule those out for the whole month. So in about an hour, so in about an hour, I could have all my videos done for the month. And if I had, once I had an outsourcer, all I would do is take that video, I'd clap, give the video guy instructions on what to do for that video, clap, make the video and clap and I'd just give it to him. We'd cut it up and schedule it so I could spend one hour, it all be scheduled, it all be done. But to make it even easier and more powerful, what I ended up doing is I would take my video and I would have my outsourcer transcribe it to a blog post and then I'd take that blog post and we'd turn it into Facebook, to Twitter, Tweets, to all these things. So from that one video came all of my content. So it looks something like this. I had the video and then we would distribute that video to the video sites. Then I had the article that came word by word from the video and we put that on the blog and article distribution. And then we put that into our newsletter and our social media and everything. So basically that one hour of answering people's questions, which is actually pretty fun, pretty easy, I would do one take, I'd never redo a take, my production quality was pretty low, but it was just about their questions and interacting with me. So from that one hour I had articles, I had social media for the whole month. So I can't believe this used to take me, this was my whole life, just this. I was doing this every day, this is everything that consumed me and now I was doing it for an hour or three hours in a month. So we would also try and create review sites because people want to go out and they want to compare your products. So occasionally we make a review site and try and control how people viewed all the products that were out there. We did a review site, we did authority interviews. Now here's what was fun about the authority interviews. Instead of doing an interview, everybody, if you got a blog post, if you got a blog and a YouTube channel, people want to be interviewed because it's free advertising for them. Everybody asked to be interviewed, I'd find somebody, oh this guy's amazing, he's never going to want to be interviewed. He loved to be interviewed. And I'd always say it like this, Jacob Hiller speaks with so and so about topic. I wouldn't say me interviewing that person because it kind of puts me below them. Now I try and put myself on the same level to make myself look like we were authorities together talking about a topic. We would do an occasional PR release and we'd start tracking our links. Hey, where's our traffic coming from? You know, is it coming from the articles, the interviews, the YouTube? So these are some tools that you can use for that. I use ad tracks myself. It's a little dated but it works for us. So three hours per week, you got your video marketing, you got your SEO, you got your blog, you got your social marketing. Some people ask me what about your keyword research? Well, I was just a one man show so what I thought at the time was hey, the verbiage that they're using to ask me questions is probably what they're typing into the search engines. So that's kind of like Hillbilly keyword marketing research, I don't know. But that's what I ended up doing and that's what I ended up working. It's just like, I didn't want to micromanage. I know there's a hundred things I could have done better and I'm almost glad I didn't know them because I might not have done anything. So I just did this. So kind of my question for you is will something like this work for you? Will something this simple? Yeah, maybe it's not the best thing in the world but it's something you can move forward with easy. Will it work for you? Will you do it? It worked for me and I'm so grateful that it did. So on your videos I just recommend do one take, just chill out, just be yourself. Good audio is probably more important than good video quality so if you can get good audio that's probably more important. You can draw on your iPad, you can have multiple cameras, do all that stuff. Make it short, sweet. And before I did my videos weren't so great because I would just give content. Hey here's some content later, you know that's it. But now I call them loops. I open the loop, here's your question, here's your answer but I don't close that question without creating what I call eternal product pain. So basically hey, here's your question, here's your answer but that's not going to solve your real problem and your real problem is this and this will. So I point to my product and let them know, yeah this is your question but you've got a lot more things to achieve and understand and do before you and let them know, kind of scratch that pain that they're really looking for. So here's what I'll show you, here's why it's important, here it is and here's what I'm not telling you and here's where you can get that. So that's kind of the structure of all my videos. Answer, question, answer and the pain. So if I think of you do this, for me it didn't happen overnight but over time it would be consistent just doing it, doing it, this momentum built and here's how I did my collaboration. I'm talking pretty fast, I don't have a ton of time so what I did is I tried to provide good tools to my affiliates. I wanted to find affiliates. People ask me where did I find my affiliates? I put it first, I was on ClickBank so ClickBank brought me a lot of affiliates and then I also, I'll show you how I went and found affiliates. So I went out to YouTube, Facebook and I found all the influencers which Russell went over to. I just found the people who could influence my jumping niche, people making videos and all that. They could be partners, products, anybody has a mutual benefit, you can find them at these places, you know where to find these people and you make a real list of them in Excel spreadsheet or have an outsourcer go and make the list and grab the emails and the places to contact them. And this is the exact email that I sent them, word for word. I said, hey cross promoting their name or their product. So I never said, hey, I get so many emails like this, I got this awesome thing, you want to promote it? Alright, maybe. But this I would talk about promoting them. Now I would say, hey I ran into your site, your product, I thought it was interesting, I wanted to see if there was an opportunity to cross promote each other. In the very least, I'd love to have you as a guest poster or on our blog or YouTube channel because really that's just free content, that's a video I don't have to make so I'm usually glad to have these people provide value to my list. So I get a lot of responses from that. I'll blast that out or send it out to these people and they will write me back. And when they write me back, I don't email them back because I want to start connecting with these people. So I get them on Skype or I get them on the phone where they can hear my voice, they can talk back and you can realize, hey, this is a real person, this is a real thing, this is not just some dude trying to get me to promote his stuff. And here's my initial launch funnel for these people, for these affiliates who wanted to work with me. I would give high value content to their list, some people will just say, hey, I want you to give me a guest blog post every week on my blog post and that drives a lot of sales to us because he's got a ton of people already, he's glad to have me, he trusts me and he just always wants more and more videos from me. I've got a few people like that. So here's the basic funnel that we used to launch to somebody's list. First time this happened to me, a guy named Larry was like, hey, I got this list of coaches, basketball coaches, big list. So I made him one value piece. I didn't make the second one yet because I wanted to hear what people said about it. The second one responded to their questions and talked to them a little more about a specific topic. For example, it would be my favorite exercise for increasing your vertical leap or how to structure the biggest three techniques or something like that. And then at the end, a limited time offer. So here's some value, here's me responding to your questions and answers and then here's a limited time two to five days offer. And I think the first time we did that with Larry, we did $30,000 in sales in a couple of days. And for me, I was like, wow, this is crazy because this guy just reached out to me. We made this funnel and that's all we did. And I tried to do two of those per month. Find somebody with a big list who was like, I'm ready to go. Use that same funnel, gave it to the same person. And even the people who didn't buy, they started to learn about us. We like ESPN called us and Sports Illustrated called us and came out and trained with us and all this stuff. It's because you're getting to these influencers and they're saying, oh, this guy is the best. They're making you look so awesome because they're promoting you and they're wanting you to look good as well. So do that two times a month. And once again, I think momentum just ends up building. So JV partners, you gather them, you send out your two to four emails per month and then you cultivate the people who are potentially interested in cross promoting each other. And I don't just say cross promote. I want to promote them to my list too on the back end. So is this something that you could do? I don't know. It ended up working for us. It didn't cost me any money. I never spent any money in the first, what, seven years of advertising my one e-book. I never spent any money. This is the kind of stuff I did. I was cheap. I was too cheap for my own good, but it happened to work out. So let me just talk to you quickly about how we're scaling now because now ClickFunnels came along and it's like cleared my mind because I could think about building a whole ad funnel without thinking about 150 pages and getting just completely mucked up. And what if they go here and all the dynamics. So for me, ClickFunnels came along for a guy who's trying to do it all solo and made me realize like it's really easy to do to build a funnel, an ad funnel. So we started doing that and our ads are cranking and I've got a great partner that I work with now who's helping me do that. So I'm no longer my own bottleneck. So all you got to do is get your cost, get what you spend, lower than what you earn. I was asked last night, what is the one thing I would change if I could do it all again? First of all, there's 100 things I'd change. But if I could say one, I would have started building this ad funnel right from the beginning because when you build an ad funnel, you pay for your traffic. So it's got to be good. It's got to convert. You got to have products on the back end. You start really honing in affiliate traffic, organic traffic is all free. So it's just all gravy. But this you really start honing on, you really start making it work. So this is our simple ad funnel. Basically we have a magnet free thing and for us it's our best exercise. What we believe is the best exercise to jump higher. And if they sign up for it, we give them a trip wire, which is a $7 offer. It's like a small guide, a PDF and a video. That upsell says five to nine, but the upsell is actually 47. So then we go to an upsell, it's 47. And then based on that, we give them, based on that, they're going to go to the list and we just do that same thing. Here's a free thing. Here's a $7 thing. Here's a 47 and onward. So it looks like this when they get into the list. We send them three emails, hey, you're interested in this? If they are, they get it and they get the flow of the upsells. If not, later on we send them another three. Okay, we've got another free thing for you. If they take it, we just do that over and over again. Try to make it as simple as possible. Yeah, we could, you know, if they clicked on this, we could do all these behavior dynamics. But this is what we did. We split tested using click funnels as much as we could. And we found out how to tweak the system and we scaled up our ad budget to about $1,000 a day and then we got banned by Facebook. And then we redid everything and now we're at it again. So it's really fun. So eventually I became the bottleneck of my own business. Just want to, some people were asking about this the other day, about how I build my team remotely. Outsourcing versus outtasking. Instead of giving a task to somebody and then another one, here's $19 or here's one task, I really love having a team. Somebody who knows my stuff, who knows what I do, this is my team. Every year I got a key to their office, I walk in on them, surprisingly, man, we go to the beach or something or hang out. I love these guys. I get my team from the Philippines. I pay them each about $400 to $600 a month. They work 40 hour weeks. So obviously virtual assistant can do a lot and these are some of the tools I know I'm kind of speeding through this. But here's what I do with my emails. First thing I love to do is get my emails out of the way. So I never look at my inbox. I only look at the emails that my outsourcer puts in the Jacob folder. And if he's supposed to do that, I'll send it back to him and say, here's how you handle that email. So now I never look at my inbox because that inbox can be a huge distraction. You look at it, just flooding in, you're just checking it, sending emails. So I never look at my inbox. I only look at the box that he has put emails specifically for me. Yeah, sometimes he sees a random personal email, whatever, but I just don't care because I love how that works for me. So I work really closely with them. Once they get the system, they just roll. They start making their own tutorials. We keep them in a file and drop box. So if we got to turn it over, hire somebody new, they can retrain themselves because the training, you think you can just hire somebody and boom, your business is outsourced, but you really got to spend time training them and then it can start duplicating and they can start duplicating themselves. But you got to spend that initial time with those people at first. Here's how I manage our workflow. I use a system called Agile or GTD. What we do is we make a huge list of every task we want to do in the world, everything I ever think of. Got an idea. I put it on the list. I bubble up our most important tasks to the top and then we do what's called a sprint. So a sprint means this week we're going to accomplish this task, this task, this task, and that one. I put them in the sprint. We name the sprint, get excited about that specific sprint. And yeah, so we do. We find that doing that is not just an infinite stream of tasks that goes forever. They have a sprint to accomplish. And I noticed there was so much more focus focusing on sprints than just the unending line of tasks. So we use Asana. That's what we use to manage our workflow. We put everybody in Asana. We name our sprint. We put the ones in for this week. We accomplished our sprint. We put a deadline on that specific sprint. So we have a group of tasks that need to be done. This is what the inbox looks like. I love the inbox in Asana because if somebody comments, you just look at your inbox, you respond to the comments, and you just keep going. You never know what am I supposed to look at today. You kind of live out of the inbox once you start working like this. So that's how I worked with my remote team. And we have the team do Pomodoro's too, which is like time boxing. So basically what they do, I like Pomodoro because Pomodoro is this idea that for 25 minutes I'm not working. I'm working 100% focused. So you take 20 minutes and you dive in. We count down and we jump into our tasks. Can we say, during these 20 minutes, I'm not allowed to look at anything else but my task. And so it's kind of a way to measure specific focus. So we use Pomodoro's. The rejects were slides that I was going to show you guys, but I won't have time. So that's it. I'm so excited about where my business is at right now. I feel so grateful that I was able to stumble through and do so many stupid things and things still worked. But I think the one thing that I did do right is I just kept doing stuff. Stuff didn't work. Stuff didn't happen. I just kept doing stuff. And maybe that's kind of my weakness too, is I'll do stuff even when I should be a little more educated. I shouldn't have started a YouTube channel if I didn't have a website and a product and all this stuff. Why would you promote something? I just kept moving forward. And I met so many people here who have all the tools that they have at all, that vision of what they want. So I'm so excited to see what you guys do with your tools and your motivation and the big vision that people are giving you here. And I appreciate you guys for listening. And thank you. Thank you. You guys keep it going. Stand up. Give it up for Jacob Hiller, ladies and gentlemen.
